questions.questionText: The hallmark cells of acute inflammation are:
questions.explanation: Acute inflammation: neutrophils predominate (first 24-48 hours). Cardinal signs: rubor (redness), calor (heat), tumor (swelling), dolor (pain), functio laesa (loss of function). Vascular changes: vasodilation → increased blood flow, increased vascular permeability → exudate. Neutrophils: first to arrive (6-24 hours), followed by monocytes/macrophages (24-48 hours). Chronic inflammation: lymphocytes, macrophages, plasma cells predominate. Reference: Robbins — Acute Inflammation.

questions.optionB: Collection of activated macrophages (epithelioid cells) ± giant cells ± central necrosis
questions.explanation: Granuloma: organized collection of epithelioid cells (activated macrophages), Langhans giant cells (horseshoe-shaped nuclei) or foreign body giant cells, surrounded by lymphocytes. Caseating granulomas: TB, fungal infections. Non-caseating: sarcoidosis (most common cause), Crohn's disease, berylliosis, leprosy (tuberculoid type). Foreign body granulomas: suture material, talc, silicone. Type IV hypersensitivity reaction. Reference: Robbins — Granulomatous Inflammation.

questions.questionText: Chemical mediators of inflammation that cause vasodilation include:
questions.optionB: Histamine, nitric oxide (NO), and prostaglandins (PGE2, PGI2)
questions.explanation: Vasodilators: Histamine (from mast cells — immediate, transient), NO (endothelium — sustained), PGE2 and PGI2 (potentiate histamine/bradykinin effect). Increased vascular permeability: histamine, bradykinin, C3a/C5a (anaphylatoxins), leukotrienes C4/D4/E4, PAF. Chemotaxis: LTB4, C5a, IL-8, bacterial products. IL-1 and TNF: acute-phase response, fever. Reference: Robbins — Mediators of Inflammation.

questions.questionText: The most important chemotactic factor for neutrophils is:
questions.explanation: Chemotactic factors for neutrophils: IL-8/CXCL8 (most potent CXC chemokine), LTB4 (leukotriene from 5-LOX pathway), C5a (complement), bacterial peptides (N-formyl-methionyl peptides like fMLP). Chemotaxis: directed migration along chemical gradient. Neutrophils follow: SELECTINS (rolling) → INTEGRINS (firm adhesion — LFA-1/Mac-1 bind ICAM-1) → DIAPEDESIS (through vessel wall) → CHEMOTAXIS (migrate to site). Reference: Robbins — Neutrophil Recruitment.

questions.optionB: Mononuclear cell infiltrate (macrophages, lymphocytes, plasma cells), tissue destruction, and fibrosis/repair
questions.explanation: Chronic inflammation: simultaneous tissue destruction and repair. Hallmarks: (1) Mononuclear infiltrate (macrophages — key cell, lymphocytes, plasma cells), (2) Tissue damage/destruction, (3) Fibrosis and angiogenesis (repair). Causes: persistent infections (TB, viral hepatitis), autoimmune diseases (RA, SLE), prolonged toxic exposure (silicosis), chronic granulomatous disease. May follow acute inflammation or arise de novo. Reference: Robbins — Chronic Inflammation.

questions.questionText: Wound healing by secondary intention differs from primary intention in that it involves:
questions.optionB: More granulation tissue, wound contraction by myofibroblasts, and greater scarring
questions.explanation: Primary intention (first intention): clean, sutured wound → minimal granulation tissue, minimal scarring. Secondary intention: large open wound, tissue loss → heals from the base with abundant granulation tissue (fibroblasts, new capillaries, loose connective tissue), wound contraction (myofibroblasts — contain α-smooth muscle actin), more inflammation, more scarring. Tertiary intention: delayed primary closure. Reference: Robbins — Wound Healing.

questions.questionText: Leukocyte adhesion deficiency type 1 (LAD-1) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: CD18 (β2 integrin subunit — LFA-1, Mac-1 deficiency)
questions.explanation: LAD-1: AR, deficiency of CD18 (β2 integrin subunit) → defective LFA-1 and Mac-1 → impaired neutrophil firm adhesion → cannot migrate to infection sites. Features: recurrent severe bacterial infections, impaired wound healing, delayed umbilical cord separation, marked leukocytosis (neutrophils stuck in blood), absence of pus formation. LAD-2: defective sialyl-Lewis X (selectin ligand) → impaired rolling. CGD: NADPH oxidase deficiency → cannot generate ROS. Reference: Robbins — LAD.

questions.questionText: The complement system participates in inflammation through:
questions.optionB: C3a/C5a (anaphylatoxins → mast cell degranulation), C5a (chemotaxis), C3b (opsonization), MAC (C5b-9, cell lysis)
questions.explanation: Complement functions: (1) C3a, C5a: anaphylatoxins — stimulate mast cell histamine release → vasodilation, permeability. C5a also potent neutrophil chemotactic factor. (2) C3b: opsonin — coats microbes for phagocytosis. (3) MAC (C5b-9): membrane attack complex → creates pores in cell membrane → lysis (especially Neisseria). Activation pathways: classical (Ag-Ab/C1q), alternative (microbial surfaces/C3b), lectin (mannose-binding). Complement deficiencies → recurrent infections. Reference: Robbins — Complement.

questions.questionText: Abscess formation is an example of:
questions.optionB: Suppurative/purulent inflammation (collection of pus — neutrophils + necrotic debris)
questions.explanation: Abscess: localized collection of pus (neutrophils, necrotic tissue, edema fluid) within a tissue, organ, or confined space. Wall of abscess = pyogenic membrane. Caused by pyogenic (pus-forming) bacteria: Staphylococcus aureus (most common cause of abscesses), Streptococci. Types of exudates: serous (clear, protein-poor — blister), fibrinous (fibrin-rich — pericarditis), suppurative/purulent (pus — abscess), hemorrhagic (blood-stained). Reference: Robbins — Suppurative Inflammation.

questions.questionText: TNF-α and IL-1 are key mediators of inflammation that cause:
questions.optionB: Fever, leukocytosis, acute-phase protein synthesis, endothelial activation, and septic shock (TNF)
questions.explanation: TNF-α and IL-1: major cytokines of acute inflammation (from activated macrophages). Effects: (1) Endothelial activation (adhesion molecule upregulation — E-selectin, ICAM-1), (2) Fever (act on hypothalamus via PGE2), (3) Acute-phase response (liver: CRP, fibrinogen, SAA), (4) Leukocytosis (bone marrow stimulation). TNF also causes: hemodynamic effects of septic shock (hypotension, DIC, metabolic derangements), cachexia in chronic diseases. Anti-TNF therapy: infliximab, adalimumab. Reference: Robbins — Cytokines.

questions.questionText: Keloid differs from a hypertrophic scar in that a keloid:
questions.optionB: Extends beyond original wound margins, does not regress, and recurs after excision
questions.explanation: Keloid: excessive scar tissue that extends BEYOND the original wound margins, does NOT regress spontaneously, tends to recur after excision. More common in dark-skinned individuals, genetic predisposition. Contains type I and type III collagen in thick hyalinized bundles. Hypertrophic scar: raised but stays WITHIN wound boundaries, may regress over time. Both result from excessive collagen deposition. Treatment: intralesional steroids, silicone sheets, radiation post-excision. Reference: Robbins — Aberrant Wound Healing.

questions.questionText: The arachidonic acid pathway enzyme targeted by NSAIDs is:
questions.explanation: NSAIDs inhibit COX (cyclooxygenase): COX-1 (constitutive — gastric protection, platelet TXA2, renal blood flow) and COX-2 (inducible — inflammation, pain, fever). Aspirin: irreversible COX inhibitor. Ibuprofen, naproxen: reversible. COX-2 selective: celecoxib (less GI toxicity but cardiovascular risk). Corticosteroids: inhibit phospholipase A2 (via lipocortin/annexin-1) → block both COX and LOX pathways. Zileuton: 5-LOX inhibitor (asthma). Reference: Robbins — Arachidonic Acid Mediators.
